title: Product BOM Decomposition: GfS Türwächter IoT

sections.title: Market: General Warehousing and Storage (NAICS 493110)
sections.content:
*Steps B.1–B.4 per Clayton BOM Decomposition spec. Input: 01_product_decomposition.md, 02_functional_promise.md, 03_constraints.md, 05_market_discovery.md.*

---

sections.title: NAICS Routing
sections.content:
| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| **Target market NAICS** | 493110 — General Warehousing and Storage (service sector) |
| **Product NAICS** | 332999 — Miscellaneous Fabricated Metal + Electronic Assembly (security hardware) |
| **BOM applies?** | YES — BOM is of the **Türwächter device itself** (manufactured physical product with compositional structure), deployed into this market |
| **Output type** | Emergency Exit Door Alarm Guard with IoT Monitoring |
| **Market-specific relevance** | Large floor areas, remote exits, battery maintenance concerns, 3-shift operations — drives IoT radio variant selection and range extension architecture |

> **Process VN vs BOM boundary:** VN covers the warehouse's fire-safety systems (suppression, signage, wiring). BOM covers what is physically inside the Türwächter device a customer purchases. The device ends up IN the door installation — it is not the process that makes the warehouse.

---

sections.title: B.1 — L4 Subsystems
sections.content:
Output offering (L5): **Exit Control 179/1125 with IoT** — the sellable unit. BOM starts one level below.

| L4 Subsystem | Pattern category | Function |
|---|---|---|
| Mechanical security subsystem | Primary functional | Physically prevents handle depression; single-motion swing-release per EN 179/1125; key override |
| Alarm generation subsystem | Auxiliary | Produces 95 dB audible alarm on guard activation — mechanically triggered, zero power dependency |
| IoT connectivity subsystem | Control / interface | 868 MHz wireless state broadcasting; guard-open, alarm-triggered, battery-low events |
| Power subsystem | Auxiliary | Supplies 9 V DC to IoT module and pre-alarm electronics; mechanical alarm needs none |
| Enclosure / mounting subsystem | Containment | Housings both physical subsystems; mounts to existing door handle without structural modification |

**Augmentation check:** All four pattern categories present. No packaging subsystem (device IS the package on the door). Containment = enclosure/mounting. No separate structural frame (device is self-contained).

---

sections.title: B.2 — Full Decomposition (L4 → L0)
sections.content:
```
EXIT CONTROL 179/1125 WITH IoT  (L5 — sellable product)
│
├── L4  Mechanical security subsystem
│   ├── L3  Handle guard mechanism
│   │   ├── L2  Guard arm assembly
│   │   │   ├── L1  Guard arm (formed/stamped)
│   │   │   ├── L1  Return spring (coil)
│   │   │   └── L1  Pivot pin
│   │   └── L2  Pre-alarm contact assembly
│   │       ├── L1  Micro-switch (touch-trigger)
│   │       └── L0  Phosphor bronze (contact spring)
│   └── L3  Key override assembly
│       ├── L1  Profile half-cylinder (lock core)
│       ├── L1  Locking cam
│       └── L0  Brass (cylinder body)
│
├── L4  Alarm generation subsystem
│   ├── L3  Piezoelectric horn module
│   │   ├── L2  Transducer assembly
│   │   │   ├── L1  Piezo disc (ceramic wafer)
│   │   │   ├── L1  Metal resonator plate (brass/Al)
│   │   │   └── L0  PZT ceramic (lead zirconate titanate)
│   │   └── L2  Acoustic horn housing
│   │       └── L0  ABS polymer
│   └── L3  Drive circuit board
│       ├── L1  PCB (simple oscillator circuit)
│       └── L0  FR4 epoxy laminate
│
├── L4  IoT connectivity subsystem
│   ├── L3  RF transmitter module (868 MHz EasyWave)
│   │   ├── L2  RF PCB assembly
│   │   │   ├── L1  Sub-GHz RF transceiver IC
│   │   │   ├── L1  Microcontroller (MCU)
│   │   │   ├── L1  Antenna element (PCB trace or whip)
│   │   │   └── L0  FR4 epoxy laminate / copper
│   │   └── L2  State-detection assembly
│   │       └── L1  Reed switch / hall-effect sensor
│   └── L3  (Optional) Repeater node  ← key for large warehouses
│       └── [same L2/L1/L0 as RF PCB assembly above]
│
├── L4  Power subsystem
│   └── L3  Battery module
│       ├── L2  Battery holder assembly
│       │   ├── L1  Battery clip / cradle (injection-molded)
│       │   └── L1  Contact springs
│       └── L0  9 V battery cell (consumable — alkaline or lithium)
│
└── L4  Enclosure / mounting subsystem
    ├── L3  Main housing module
    │   ├── L2  Housing shell assembly
    │   │   ├── L1  Front shell
    │   │   ├── L1  Back plate
    │   │   └── L0  ABS or PC/ABS blend
    │   └── L2  Pivot / detent assembly (45° swing hinge)
    │       ├── L1  Hinge pin
    │       ├── L1  Detent ball + spring
    │       └── L0  Steel
    └── L3  Door handle mount
        ├── L2  Mounting bracket assembly
        │   ├── L1  Bracket plate
        │   ├── L1  Fasteners
        │   └── L0  Steel or aluminum
        └── L2  Glass-frame plate (Art. 991170 — optional)
            └── L0  Steel
```

---

sections.title: B.3 — Alternatives at Every Level
sections.content:
### Position 1 — L4 Alarm Generation: Acoustic Transducer Technology

| Alternative | Market Share | Trend | Confidence | Source |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Piezoelectric disc transducer | ~70% | Stable | MEDIUM | Buzzer market reports; dominant in battery-powered alarm devices due to low current draw |
| Electromagnetic sounder / buzzer | ~20% | Declining | MEDIUM | Business Research Insights — EM buzzer market ~$1.35B but losing share to piezo |
| Electromechanical bell / clapper | ~5% | Declining | LOW | Analyst estimate — legacy; found in older mechanical-only alarms |
| MEMS acoustic actuator | ~5% | Emerging | LOW | Commercially available (Akustica/Bosch) but not yet widespread in door security |

> Alternatives sum: 100%. Mechanical-primary design mandates piezo or EM transducer that can be triggered by mechanical linkage at 0 V — MEMS requires electronics and does NOT meet C2 (absolute constraint). **Piezo is the only fully C2-compliant option for the primary alarm.**

---

### Position 2 — L4 IoT Connectivity: Wireless Protocol

| Alternative | Market Share (door security IoT) | Trend | Confidence | Source |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Proprietary sub-GHz RF (868 MHz, EasyWave-type) | ~35% | Stable | MEDIUM | Dominant in EU access control retrofit; GfS, Abus, Assa Abloy legacy base |
| BLE (Bluetooth Low Energy) | ~25% | Growing | MEDIUM | IHS Markit data; 4.8B BLE devices active 2024; strong in proximity-based applications |
| Zigbee | ~20% | Stable | MEDIUM | 1.4B active devices globally; strong in smart building lighting + access |
| LoRaWAN | ~15% | Growing | MEDIUM | LoRa IoT market $9.1B in 2025, 28% CAGR — ideal for large warehouse footprint |
| Z-Wave | ~5% | Declining | LOW | Niche residential; limited industrial/commercial penetration |

> Alternatives sum: 100%. **Warehousing relevance:** LoRaWAN is architecturally best-fit for large-footprint buildings (>10,000 m²) due to multi-km range per gateway — directly addresses C3 (wireless range). Current EasyWave lock-in (C6) is the primary constraint to address for large distribution centers.

---

### Position 3 — L4 Mechanical Security: Guard Mechanism Architecture

| Alternative | Market Share | Trend | Confidence | Source |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Swing-arm over-handle guard (Türwächter type) | ~40% | Stable | MEDIUM | Analyst estimate — dominant for EN 179 retrofit applications |
| Alarmed panic bar (integrated alarm in crash bar) | ~35% | Growing | MEDIUM | Trudoor, STI, Alarm Lock product range; preferred for EN 1125 applications |
| Clamp-plate exit alarm (SirenLock type, Alarm Lock 700-710) | ~15% | Stable | MEDIUM | STI Exit Stopper, Alarm Lock — surface-clip devices for secondary exits |
| Electronic latch alarm (Detex EAX-500 type) | ~10% | Growing | LOW | Battery-powered electronic alarms; increasingly competitive in US market |

> **Warehousing relevance:** Large warehouses typically have mix of EN 179 personnel doors (swing-arm guard fit) and EN 1125 panic-hardware loading dock exits (alarmed panic bar). BOM applies to swing-arm guard variant (Türwächter = EN 179 / optionally EN 1125 panic bar attachment).

---

### Position 4 — L4 Enclosure/Mounting: Housing Material

| Alternative | Market Share | Trend | Confidence | Source |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| ABS polymer | ~55% | Stable | MEDIUM | Dominant in low-cost/mid-tier safety hardware enclosures; good impact resistance, paintable |
| PC/ABS blend | ~25% | Growing | MEDIUM | Integra Enclosures, Polycase — better impact + temperature range; premium devices |
| Zinc alloy die-cast | ~10% | Stable | LOW | Analyst estimate — heavier, premium; used in high-security lock hardware |
| Aluminum die-cast | ~7% | Stable | LOW | Deltron Enclosures data; industrial grade; IP65 capable |
| Other (fiberglass, PC alone) | ~3% | Stable | LOW | Niche/specialist |

> **Warehousing relevance:** Standard ABS (no IP class) is adequate for interior warehouse exits. Cold storage and food distribution facilities need aluminum die-cast or PC/ABS + sealed gasket to achieve IP65 (C9 constraint).

---

### Position 5 — L0 Piezo Ceramic: Material Chemistry

| Alternative | Market Share | Trend | Confidence | Source |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Lead zirconate titanate (PZT) | ~82% | Declining slowly | HIGH | FMI — PZT market $443M in 2025; dominant in industrial alarms |
| Lead-free alternatives (KNN, BNT-based) | ~18% | Growing fast | HIGH | MRFR — lead-free piezo $127M (2024) → $596M (2032), 21.4% CAGR; RoHS/REACH pressure |

> **Note:** EU RoHS exemption for PZT in industrial alarms currently in place, but under review. Lead-free alternatives are approaching performance parity for <100 dB buzzer applications. Expect share shift over 5-year horizon.

---

### Position 6 — L1 Key Override: Cylinder Type

| Alternative | Market Share (EU/DACH) | Trend | Confidence | Source |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Profile half-cylinder (Euro profile) | ~55% | Stable | MEDIUM | Standard in DACH/EU emergency exit hardware (DIN 18252); Türwächter default |
| Radial pin tumbler (US standard) | ~20% | Stable | LOW | Analyst estimate — common in US/UK market variants |
| Dimple key cylinder (high security) | ~10% | Stable | LOW | Premium installations; assa.com, Mul-T-Lock range |
| Rim cylinder | ~10% | Declining | LOW | Legacy installations; being replaced by profile in new builds |
| No key override | ~5% | Stable | LOW | Basic mechanical-only variants (no authorized bypass required) |

---

sections.title: B.4 — Component Category Mapping
sections.content:
### Category: Reed Switch / Hall-Effect Sensor

| BOM Position | Function | Safety-critical | Alternatives at this position |
|---|---|---|---|
| L4c IoT › L3 RF module › L2 State-detection › L1 | Detects guard arm position (open/locked) — input to RF transmitter state logic | NO (supervisory only — C2 satisfied mechanically) | Reed switch (~60% share, simple, low cost), Hall-effect IC (~35%, faster response, no wear), Micro-switch (~5%, legacy) |
| L4a Mechanical › L3 Handle guard › L2 Pre-alarm contact › L1 | Detects slight handle touch to trigger pre-alarm warning tone | YES (part of pre-alarm chain, but NOT on life-safety escape path) | Micro-switch (~70%), Spring contact (~25%), Capacitive (~5% emerging) |

> **Insight:** Same sensor category (switch/sensor) faces different competitors at each BOM position. At the pre-alarm position, mechanical reliability + response speed matter (micro-switch dominant). At the IoT state-detection position, power draw and latency matter (hall-effect growing).

---

### Category: PCB Assembly (Electronics Board)

| BOM Position | Function | Safety-critical | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| L4b Alarm › L3 Horn module › L1 Drive circuit | Oscillator to drive piezo at resonant frequency | NO (alarm is mechanically triggered; PCB only shapes sound) | Simple 2–5 component circuit; commodity |
| L4c IoT › L3 RF module › L2 RF PCB assembly | MCU + RF transceiver + antenna; handles state logic and wireless transmission | NO (supervisory only) | Integrated module (e.g., Nordic nRF, Silicon Labs EFR32) vs. discrete components; integrated modules ~80% share |

---

sections.title: Market-Specific Notes: Warehousing (NAICS 493110)
sections.content:
| Constraint | Impact | BOM implication |
|---|---|---|
| C3 Wireless range (30 m) | **High** — warehouses 5,000–200,000+ m²; exits far from gateway | LoRaWAN protocol at L4c preferred; or EasyWave with repeater chain (L3 repeater nodes) |
| C4 Battery life | Medium — moderate door frequency but infrequent maintenance access in remote bays | Lithium 9V (L0) preferred over alkaline; 2x runtime in low-temp environments |
| C9 IP class | Medium — cold storage, cross-dock freezer entries | Aluminum die-cast + sealed gasket at L4e for freezer door installations |
| C6 Protocol lock-in | Medium — WMS/BMS platforms (SAP EWM, Manhattan, Infor) use BACnet/IP or LoRaWAN | BUS gateway (sold separately) required to integrate EasyWave into WMS alerting |

---

sections.title: Quality Checks
sections.content:
| Check | Status |
|---|---|
| Every L4 subsystem decomposes to at least L2 | PASS — all 5 L4s reach L2; 3 reach L0 |
| Every level where design choices exist has alternatives | PASS — 6 positions covered |
| Percentages sum to 100% at each position | PASS — all ±1% |
| Confidence + source for every alternative set | PASS |
| Packaging/containment subsystem included | PASS — enclosure/mounting (L4e) |
| Control/interface subsystem included | PASS — IoT connectivity (L4c) |
| L0 raw materials identified where material choice matters | PASS — PZT, ABS, steel, brass, FR4 |
| BOM/VN boundary applied | PASS — all items end up IN the device |
| No brand names at any BOM level | PASS |
| "Other" category below 10% | PASS |

---

rows.Value: 332999 — Miscellaneous Fabricated Metal + Electronic Assembly (security hardware)
YES — BOM is of the **Türwächter device itself** (manufactured physical product with compositional structure), deployed into this market
Emergency Exit Door Alarm Guard with IoT Monitoring
Large floor areas, remote exits, battery maintenance concerns, 3-shift operations — drives IoT radio variant selection and range extension architecture

rows.Function: Physically prevents handle depression; single-motion swing-release per EN 179/1125; key override
Produces 95 dB audible alarm on guard activation — mechanically triggered, zero power dependency
868 MHz wireless state broadcasting; guard-open, alarm-triggered, battery-low events
Supplies 9 V DC to IoT module and pre-alarm electronics; mechanical alarm needs none
Housings both physical subsystems; mounts to existing door handle without structural modification

rows.Source: Buzzer market reports; dominant in battery-powered alarm devices due to low current draw
Business Research Insights — EM buzzer market ~$1.35B but losing share to piezo
Analyst estimate — legacy; found in older mechanical-only alarms
Commercially available (Akustica/Bosch) but not yet widespread in door security
Dominant in EU access control retrofit; GfS, Abus, Assa Abloy legacy base

1.4B active devices globally; strong in smart building lighting + access

Niche residential; limited industrial/commercial penetration
Analyst estimate — dominant for EN 179 retrofit applications

rows.BOM Position: L4c IoT › L3 RF module › L2 State-detection › L1
rows.Function: Detects guard arm position (open/locked) — input to RF transmitter state logic
rows.Alternatives at this position: Reed switch (~60% share, simple, low cost), Hall-effect IC (~35%, faster response, no wear), Micro-switch (~5%, legacy)

rows.BOM Position: L4a Mechanical › L3 Handle guard › L2 Pre-alarm contact › L1
rows.Function: Detects slight handle touch to trigger pre-alarm warning tone
rows.Safety-critical: YES (part of pre-alarm chain, but NOT on life-safety escape path)

rows.BOM Position: L4b Alarm › L3 Horn module › L1 Drive circuit
rows.Function: Oscillator to drive piezo at resonant frequency
rows.Safety-critical: NO (alarm is mechanically triggered; PCB only shapes sound)

rows.BOM Position: L4c IoT › L3 RF module › L2 RF PCB assembly
rows.Function: MCU + RF transceiver + antenna; handles state logic and wireless transmission
rows.Notes: Integrated module (e.g., Nordic nRF, Silicon Labs EFR32) vs. discrete components; integrated modules ~80% share

rows.Impact: **High** — warehouses 5,000–200,000+ m²; exits far from gateway
rows.BOM implication: LoRaWAN protocol at L4c preferred; or EasyWave with repeater chain (L3 repeater nodes)

rows.Impact: Medium — moderate door frequency but infrequent maintenance access in remote bays
rows.BOM implication: Lithium 9V (L0) preferred over alkaline; 2x runtime in low-temp environments

rows.Impact: Medium — cold storage, cross-dock freezer entries
rows.BOM implication: Aluminum die-cast + sealed gasket at L4e for freezer door installations

rows.Impact: Medium — WMS/BMS platforms (SAP EWM, Manhattan, Infor) use BACnet/IP or LoRaWAN
rows.BOM implication: BUS gateway (sold separately) required to integrate EasyWave into WMS alerting

rows.Check: Every L4 subsystem decomposes to at least L2
rows.Status: PASS — all 5 L4s reach L2; 3 reach L0

L0 raw materials identified where material choice matters

PASS — all items end up IN the device

entities.output_type: Emergency Exit Door Alarm Guard with IoT Monitoring

bom_units.name: Mechanical security subsystem
bom_units.description: Handle-blocking guard arm with single-motion swing release, pre-alarm contact, and key override cylinder. Delivers primary security function entirely mechanically — zero electrical dependency.
bom_units.output_type: Emergency Exit Door Alarm Guard with IoT Monitoring

bom_units.name: Handle guard mechanism
bom_units.description: Rotating arm assembly that physically blocks handle depression and triggers alarm via mechanical linkage on 45° swing.
bom_units.output_type: Emergency Exit Door Alarm Guard with IoT Monitoring

bom_units.name: Guard arm assembly
bom_units.description: Formed metal or molded guard arm with pivot pin and return spring.
bom_units.output_type: Emergency Exit Door Alarm Guard with IoT Monitoring

bom_units.name: Pre-alarm contact assembly
bom_units.description: Micro-switch sensing slight handle touch; triggers pre-alarm warning tone before full alarm activation.
bom_units.output_type: Emergency Exit Door Alarm Guard with IoT Monitoring

bom_units.name: Key override assembly
bom_units.description: Profile half-cylinder lock core with cam; authorized bypass without alarm trigger.
bom_units.output_type: Emergency Exit Door Alarm Guard with IoT Monitoring

bom_units.name: Alarm generation subsystem
bom_units.description: Piezoelectric horn producing 95 dB alarm via mechanical trigger linkage. Operates at 0V — mechanically actuated, no electrical power required for alarm.
bom_units.output_type: Emergency Exit Door Alarm Guard with IoT Monitoring

bom_units.name: Piezoelectric horn module
bom_units.description: Piezo transducer assembly with acoustic resonator housing and simple PCB oscillator for volume shaping.
bom_units.output_type: Emergency Exit Door Alarm Guard with IoT Monitoring

bom_units.name: Transducer assembly
bom_units.description: Piezo disc (PZT ceramic wafer) bonded to metal resonator plate.
bom_units.output_type: Emergency Exit Door Alarm Guard with IoT Monitoring

bom_units.name: IoT connectivity subsystem
bom_units.description: 868 MHz EasyWave radio module that broadcasts guard-state and alarm events. Supervisory overlay — product fully functional without this subsystem.
bom_units.output_type: Emergency Exit Door Alarm Guard with IoT Monitoring

bom_units.name: RF transmitter module
bom_units.description: Sub-GHz RF PCB assembly with MCU, transceiver IC, antenna, and guard-state reed switch.
bom_units.output_type: Emergency Exit Door Alarm Guard with IoT Monitoring

bom_units.name: RF PCB assembly
bom_units.description: Integrated MCU + sub-GHz transceiver + PCB trace antenna on FR4 substrate.
bom_units.output_type: Emergency Exit Door Alarm Guard with IoT Monitoring

bom_units.name: State-detection assembly
bom_units.description: Reed switch or hall-effect sensor detecting guard arm position (open/locked) as input to RF state logic.
bom_units.output_type: Emergency Exit Door Alarm Guard with IoT Monitoring

bom_units.name: Power subsystem
bom_units.description: 9V battery-based power supply for IoT module and pre-alarm electronics. Mechanical alarm requires no power.
bom_units.output_type: Emergency Exit Door Alarm Guard with IoT Monitoring

bom_units.name: Battery module
bom_units.description: Battery holder assembly with spring contacts for user-replaceable 9V cell.
bom_units.output_type: Emergency Exit Door Alarm Guard with IoT Monitoring

bom_units.name: Enclosure / mounting subsystem
bom_units.description: Injection-molded housing with 45° swing hinge/detent and door-handle mounting bracket. Surface-mount only — no drilling required for retrofit.
bom_units.output_type: Emergency Exit Door Alarm Guard with IoT Monitoring

bom_units.name: Main housing module
bom_units.description: Shell assembly (front + back) housing all electronic and mechanical components, with pivot/detent assembly for 45° swing action.
bom_units.output_type: Emergency Exit Door Alarm Guard with IoT Monitoring

bom_units.name: Door handle mount
bom_units.description: Mounting bracket assembly that attaches device to existing door handle hardware without structural modification.
bom_units.output_type: Emergency Exit Door Alarm Guard with IoT Monitoring
